title.emphasis: Part IV: A Unified Theory of Coercion: From Interpersonal Threats to Structural Power
4.1 The Legal Standard: Coercion as the Overcoming of Will

section.para: The legal framework for affirmative consent is built to identify and prohibit overt acts of coercion. In the context of sexual assault law, coercion is not merely a feeling of being pressured; it is a specific set of actions that negates consent by overpowering a person's will.25 The law is not concerned with whether a person felt awkward or reluctant, but whether their ability to freely choose was taken from them. Legally, coercion is established through
Force, Violence, or Duress, which involves the use of physical force or the threat of immediate bodily harm to compel submission.25 It can also be accomplished through
Threats and Fear of future harm, which must be credible and sufficient to place a reasonable person in fear for their safety or the safety of others.27 Finally,
Intimidation and Abuse of Authority can create an environment where a subordinate feels they have no real choice but to comply due to a significant power imbalance.29

The law makes a critical distinction between these coercive tactics and common social behaviors like persuasion or seduction. Coercion focuses on the consequences of saying "no," framing the choice around avoiding a negative outcome (e.g., "If you don't have sex with me, I will evict you").25 Persuasion focuses on the benefits of saying "yes," aiming to make an option more attractive (e.g., "If we are intimate, our emotional connection will grow").25 While persistent pleading can be ethically questionable, it does not rise to the level of legal coercion unless it crosses into harassment or threats that make a person fear the consequences of refusal. This high, objective standard is necessary to distinguish criminal conduct from the often-messy dynamics of human influence in intimate relationships.25

title.emphasis: 4.2 Systemic Coercion: How the Prohibitionist State Precludes Affirmative Consent

section.para: The established legal definition of coercion focuses on an interpersonal model: one person acts to overpower the will of another.25 This model, however, is blind to a more insidious form of pressure: systemic coercion, where the legal framework itself creates the very atmosphere of fear it claims to forbid.30 The criminalization of prostitution under PC § 647(b) does not merely forbid a transaction; it creates a field of legal peril around any relationship that blends intimacy with financial support. This pervasive threat of criminalization—of allegation, arrest, and prosecution—functions as a structural threat that hangs over any such relationship. The state itself becomes the source of the threat that distorts free choice.

This "coercive shadow" makes the standard of affirmative consent a logical and practical impossibility. First, it mandates obscurity over clarity. Affirmative consent requires unambiguous communication, yet the threat of prosecution forces individuals into "workarounds" designed to conceal the nature of their agreement.7 One cannot give clear, affirmative consent to the terms of a relationship when the law compels both parties to systematically misrepresent those very terms.

Second, it replaces volition with fear. A voluntary choice is one made without fear of illegitimate consequences. The prohibitionist framework injects a constant fear of catastrophic legal consequences, constraining every decision within the relationship by the need to avoid legal jeopardy.

Third, it creates a coercive power imbalance. The law becomes a weapon that can be wielded by either party or a malicious third party. A person may feel compelled to agree to acts not out of genuine desire, but out of fear that a refusal could lead to a vindictive and legally credible accusation of prostitution. This dynamic mirrors the logic of interpersonal coercion—"agree, or face a terrible consequence"—but outsources the enforcement of the consequence to the state.

This creates an irreconcilable paradox: the state demands that all sexual acts be based on affirmative consent, which requires an absence of fear and coercion, while simultaneously defining a category of relationships where its own laws create a permanent condition of fear and coercion. The state, therefore, demands a standard of consent while actively creating the conditions that make it impossible for some people to meet that standard. As long as the coercive shadow of criminalization exists, the possibility of genuine, legally recognizable affirmative consent within these relationships remains out of reach.

title.emphasis: 4.3 Structural Coercion: Economic Disparity, Promissory Estoppel, and the Impossibility of Choice

section.para: Beyond the coercion created by the legal system, structural coercion can arise from profound inequalities within the relationship itself.37 This form of coercion operates not by creating fear of a new harm, but by leveraging the fear of losing access to essential stability and well-being. When one person is entirely financially dependent on another for basic needs—such as housing, food, and healthcare—their choices are fundamentally constrained.56 The "freedom" to say no to a sexual advance is not a real choice if exercising that freedom means risking homelessness or destitution. The threat does not need to be spoken; the dependent party understands that their material security is contingent on the satisfaction of the dominant party.

This dynamic can be further manipulated through the principle of promissory estoppel, where a promise of future security—a house, financial stability, career support—is used as a tool of control. The dependent person's consent to intimacy may be predicated on the belief that these promises will be fulfilled, a form of "conditional consent." The power to withdraw the promise becomes a coercive tool, exploiting the dependent partner's hope and turning their own aspirations into a lever of control.

The concept of "Relational Autonomy" provides the theoretical key to understanding why structural coercion is so damaging.8 Traditional models of autonomy focus on an individual making a rational choice free from interference. Relational autonomy, emerging from feminist bioethics, posits that true autonomy is a capacity that is socially and materially sustained. A person's ability to form authentic preferences and make self-directed choices is developed and maintained through supportive relationships and environmental conditions.8 Structural coercion destroys these conditions. Through economic dependency and manipulative promises, it systematically erodes the foundations of security, support, and self-worth. Therefore, even if a person gives formal "consent" in a structurally coercive relationship, that consent is ethically and psychologically invalid because it is not the product of a relationally autonomous self. The law's inability to see this—its focus only on overt, interpersonal threats—is a failure to protect the very foundation of meaningful consent. In such situations, it is plausible to argue that it is, in principle, impossible for the dependent person to give true affirmative consent. Their will is constrained not by an illegal act, but by the very structure of their existence within the relationship.
